explanation.title: Storing and Processing Multiple Values with Java Arrays

codeExample.title: Class Score Report
codeExample.explanation:
- `scores[0]` — zero-based indexing; the first element is at index 0, the last at `length - 1`
- `for (int i = 0; i < scores.length; i++)` — classic for loop used because we need the index `i` for both arrays
- `scores[i] > highest` — comparison inside the loop; updates `highest` and `topName` together when a new high is found
- `(double) total / scores.length` — casting to `double` forces decimal division; without it, integer division truncates
- `%-10s` in printf — left-aligns the string in a 10-character field; the minus sign flips the default right-alignment

exercise.title: Compute Array Statistics
exercise.instructions:
Given int[] data = {14, 38, 7, 56, 23, 45, 9}, write code to compute: sum of all elements, the minimum value, the maximum value, and the average as a double. Print all four results on separate labelled lines using printf. Use a single for-each loop to compute sum, min, and max together.

Expected output:
Sum: 192
Min: 7
Max: 56
Average: 27.4
hints: 💡 Print min: System.out.println("Min: " + min); and max: System.out.println("Max: " + max);
💡 Print average with printf: System.out.printf("Average: %.1f%n", average); to get exactly one decimal place.
💡 The loop logic is already complete — you only need to add the three missing print statements.

questions.question: What index holds the first element of a Java array?
options: The length of the array
questions.explanation: Java arrays use zero-based indexing. The first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and the last element at index array.length - 1. Attempting to access an index equal to or greater than array.length throws an ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException at runtime.

questions.question: How do you get the number of elements in a Java array?
options: arr.size()
arr.count()
arr.length
arr.length()
questions.explanation: For arrays in Java, the element count is accessed via the .length field — no parentheses because it is a property, not a method. Compare this with String where you call .length() with parentheses (it is a method). ArrayList and other collections use .size().

explanation.title: Dynamic Collections with Java's ArrayList
explanation.content:
# Limitations of Arrays

Java arrays have a fixed size — once created, you cannot add or remove elements. If you do not know the final count in advance, or if the collection grows as the program runs, you need a more flexible data structure.

## ArrayList

`ArrayList` is a resizable list from `java.util`. It grows automatically as you add elements:

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;

ArrayList<String> names = new ArrayList<>();
names.add("Rahul");
names.add("Priya");
names.add("Amit");
System.out.println(names.size());   // 3
System.out.println(names.get(0));   // Rahul
```

The `<String>` in angle brackets is a **type parameter** — it tells Java what type of objects the list holds.

## Common ArrayList Methods

| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| `.add(element)` | Appends to the end |
| `.add(index, element)` | Inserts at position |
| `.get(index)` | Returns element at index |
| `.set(index, element)` | Replaces element at index |
| `.remove(index)` | Removes by index |
| `.remove(object)` | Removes first occurrence of object |
| `.size()` | Returns count of elements |
| `.contains(element)` | Returns true if element present |
| `.indexOf(element)` | Returns first index or -1 |
| `.clear()` | Removes all elements |
| `.isEmpty()` | Returns true if size is 0 |

## Iterating Over an ArrayList

```java
// Enhanced for — most readable
for (String name : names) {
    System.out.println(name);
}

// Classic for — use when you need the index
for (int i = 0; i < names.size(); i++) {
    System.out.println(i + ": " + names.get(i));
}
```

## ArrayList vs Array — When to Use Which

| Situation | Use |
|---|---|
| Fixed size, known at creation | `int[]` array |
| Size changes at runtime | `ArrayList<Integer>` |
| Primitive performance critical | Array |
| Need .contains(), .indexOf() | ArrayList |

In the code example below, you will see an ArrayList managing a dynamic student roster with add, remove, and search operations.

codeExample.title: Dynamic Student Roster
codeExample.explanation:
- `ArrayList<String> roster = new ArrayList<>()` — the type parameter `<String>` ensures only Strings can be added
- `.add("Kavitha Nair")` — appends to the end; the list grows automatically
- `.remove("Amit Patel")` — removes the first matching element; returns `true` if found
- `.indexOf(search)` — returns the zero-based position; `idx + 1` converts to 1-based for display
- `for (String name : roster)` — the enhanced for loop works with ArrayList just like arrays
- `.startsWith("P")` — String method used inside the loop to filter matching elements

exercise.title: Build a Course Enrolment Manager
exercise.instructions:
Create an ArrayList<String> named courses. Add these four courses: 'Python', 'JavaScript', 'Java', 'HTML/CSS'. Print the count using size(). Check if 'Java' is in the list using contains() and print the result. Remove 'Python' and print the updated list using a for-each loop. Add 'React' and print the final count.

Expected output includes size 4, Java present true, Python removed, and final size 4.
hints: 💡 Add a for-each loop: for (String c : courses) { System.out.println("  " + c); }
💡 After adding React, print: System.out.println("Final count: " + courses.size());
💡 ArrayList uses .size() (not .length) to return the number of elements.

questions.question: What is the key advantage of ArrayList over a regular Java array?
options: ArrayList holds primitive types directly; arrays require wrappers
ArrayList can grow and shrink dynamically; arrays have a fixed size set at creation
ArrayList is faster than arrays for all operations
ArrayList can hold multiple types simultaneously; arrays cannot
questions.explanation: A Java array has a fixed size determined when it is created with new int[5] — you cannot add or remove elements. ArrayList dynamically resizes as you call add() and remove(). This makes ArrayList ideal when the number of elements is not known in advance or changes during program execution.

questions.question: How do you access the element at index 2 in an ArrayList named list?
options: list[2]
list.element(2)
list.get(2)
list.fetch(2)
questions.explanation: ArrayList elements are accessed with the .get(index) method. Unlike arrays (which use arr[index] bracket notation), ArrayList requires a method call. list.get(2) returns the element at zero-based index 2. Attempting bracket notation on an ArrayList causes a compile error.

explanation.title: Building a Complete Student Grade System Using All Core Java Concepts
explanation.content:
# The Mini Project

You have now learned every core building block of Java programming:

1. **Java structure** — class, main method, output, comments
2. **Variables and types** — primitives, String, casting, parsing
3. **Operators** — arithmetic, comparison, logical, ternary
4. **Conditions** — if/else if/else, switch
5. **Loops** — for, for-each, while, break, continue
6. **Methods** — declaration, parameters, return values, overloading
7. **Arrays** — creation, access, iteration, common patterns
8. **ArrayList** — dynamic collections, add, remove, search

In this lesson, you will build a **Student Grade System** — a complete program that stores student records, computes statistics, ranks students, and prints a formatted report. Every concept from every module is used here.

## Design: What the System Must Do

- Store each student's name and three subject scores
- Compute average for each student
- Assign a letter grade based on average
- Find the top student
- Compute class average
- Print a formatted ranked report

## Data Structure Choice

We use **parallel arrays** — separate arrays for names and scores that are linked by index. Student at `names[i]` has scores at `scores[i]`.

```java
String[] names  = {"Rahul", "Priya", ...};
int[][]  scores = {{85, 72, 90}, {92, 88, 95}, ...};
```

## Method Decomposition

Breaking the problem into focused methods:

- `computeAverage(int[])` — average of a student's three scores
- `assignGrade(double)` — letter grade for an average
- `findTopStudent(String[], int[][])` — name of highest-average student
- `printReport(String[], int[][], ...)` — formatted table output

This decomposition mirrors real professional Java code. Each method is independently testable and reusable.

In the code example below, you will see the complete Student Grade System implemented using every concept from this course. Study each method before reading the main method.

codeExample.title: Complete Student Grade System
codeExample.explanation:
- `for (int s : subjectScores) total += s` — for-each over a method parameter array; accumulates the total
- `(double) total / subjectScores.length` — cast before dividing to force decimal division
- `int[][] scores` — a 2D array; each row is one student's three subject scores
- `computeAverage(scores[i])` — passing a row of the 2D array to a method that accepts `int[]`
- `%-10s %4s %4s %4d %7.1f %5s` — format string with width/alignment codes for a neat table layout
- `countPassing` defined after `main` — Java allows methods in any order within a class

exercise.title: Extend the Grade System with a Remark
exercise.instructions:
Add a static method getRemark(String grade) to the grade system that returns: 'Excellent!' for A, 'Well done!' for B, 'Keep going!' for C, and 'Seek help.' for F. Then add a loop in main that prints each student's name, grade, and remark on one line. Use the same names and scores arrays from the code example.

Expected output includes lines like: Priya A Excellent!
hints: 💡 Write getRemark using a switch: switch (grade) { case "A": return "Excellent!"; case "B": return "Well done!"; ... default: return "Seek help."; }
💡 Inside the loop, call: String remark = getRemark(grade); then print: System.out.println(names[i] + " " + grade + " " + remark);
💡 grade is already computed earlier in the loop — pass it directly to getRemark(grade).

questions.question: Why is breaking a large program into multiple static methods better than writing everything in main?
options: Multiple methods run faster than a single main method
Java requires at least five methods in every class
Each method can be tested independently and the logic is easier to read and reuse
Static methods use less memory than the code inside main
questions.explanation: Decomposing a program into focused methods makes each piece independently testable, easier to understand (a well-named method communicates intent), and reusable across the codebase. A change to one method only affects callers of that method. This is the Single Responsibility Principle — each method does one thing well.

questions.question: How does passing int[][] scores[i] to a method that accepts int[] work?
options: It does not work — a 2D array cannot be passed to a 1D array parameter
scores[i] is one row of the 2D array, which is a regular int[] — an exact type match
Java automatically converts the entire 2D array to 1D when passed to a method
The method receives a copy of the entire 2D array, not just one row
questions.explanation: A 2D array in Java is an array of arrays. scores is an int[][] — an array of int[] rows. scores[i] accesses the i-th row, which is a regular int[] object. Passing scores[i] to a method that accepts int[] is a perfect type match — the method receives the row as a normal single-dimensional array.

questions.question: Which six Java concepts did the Student Grade System mini project combine?
options: GUI, networking, file I/O, databases, threads, and encryption
Variables and types, conditions, loops, methods, arrays, and output formatting
Classes, inheritance, polymorphism, interfaces, exceptions, and generics
Recursion, sorting algorithms, searching algorithms, trees, graphs, and hashing
questions.explanation: The Student Grade System used: typed variables and arrays (Module 1-2), if/else conditions and switch for grading (Module 3), for and for-each loops to process data (Module 4), static methods with parameters and return values (Module 5), and 2D arrays with printf formatting (Module 6). These are the exact six beginner building blocks of Java.
